name: Nordic Lowlands (Denmark / Netherlands)

staple_foods.name: lowland dairy butter (conventional/mixed feed)

primary_metabolites.palmitic_acid: 27-32% of total fatty acids [Danish lowland butter — van Amburgh et al. 2015 J Dairy Sci 98:7447]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 20-24% of total fatty acids [conventional Danish butter — van Amburgh et al. 2015]
primary_metabolites.stearic_acid: 9-12% of total fatty acids [conventional Danish butter]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 1.5-2.2% of total fatty acids [conventional Danish butter — van Amburgh et al. 2015]

key_flavor_bioactives.conjugated_linoleic_acid: 0.6-1.5 g/100g fat [conventional lowland Danish/Dutch butter CLA — Collomb et al. 2002 J Dairy Sci 85:1239; lowest CLA in the butter natural experiment; year-round indoor feeding with silage/grain supplementation]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 12-22 mg/kg [conventional lowland butter — lowest tocopherol in butter experiment; grain feed has less vitamin E than fresh alpine herb grass]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 3-8 mg GAE/100g [conventional butter — trace from silage-based feed]

staple_foods.notes: Nordic lowland dairy butter — the low-CLA baseline anchor of the butter natural experiment. Danish and Dutch butter from Holstein-Friesian cows in intensive lowland dairy systems with indoor winter housing and silage/grain supplementation. CLA content (0.6-1.5 g/100g fat) is the lowest in the butter CLA gradient: RSU-43 lowland 30m ~0.6-1.5 g → RSU-37 Irish Atlantic 100m ~2-3.5 g → RSU-36 Alpine 1200m ~3-5.5 g → RSU-42 Himalayan yak 3800m ~3.5-6.5 g. The diet composition (silage/grain vs fresh alpine grass herbs) is the principal driver of the CLA gradient — altitude is correlated with forage quality rather than being directly causal.
data_sources: Collomb et al. (2002) — Conjugated linoleic acids and trans fatty acids in alpine and lowland milk, J Dairy Sci 85:1239
van Amburgh et al. (2015) — Dairy cow feed and milk fatty acid composition, J Dairy Sci 98:7447

staple_foods.name: rye bread (rugbrød, Danish sourdough rye)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 400-700 mg GAE/100g [Danish rugbrød — ferulic acid dominant; Andersson et al. 2010 Food Chem 119:240]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.6-1.4 g/100g [Danish rugbrød sourdough — strong LAB acidification; Arendt et al. 2007]
organic_acids.acetic_acid: 0.3-0.6 g/100g [rugbrød — higher acetic than wheat sourdough]

staple_foods.cultivar: Secale cereale (Danish whole-rye, high-fiber rugbrød varieties)
staple_foods.notes: Danish rugbrød — the most fiber-dense mainstream bread in the world. 100% whole rye sourdough, dense texture, intensely fermented LAB profile. The strong acetic acid signal distinguishes it from other sourdough breads in the dataset. Eaten daily by Danes as smørrebrød (open-faced sandwich) base.
data_sources: Andersson et al. (2010) — Phenolic antioxidants in rye bread, Food Chem 119:240

staple_foods.name: North Sea herring (pickled/fermented)

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 11-16% of total fatty acids [Atlantic herring — Ackman & Takeuchi 1986 Lipids 21:117]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 1-2% of total fatty acids [herring — very low plant omega-6]

key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 1.5-2.5 mg/100g [herring — significant vitamin E; Ackman & Takeuchi 1986]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.3-0.8 g/100g [pickled/matjes herring LAB fermentation during salt-maturing]

staple_foods.notes: North Sea herring — the foundational protein-fat of Nordic coastal food culture. Matjes herring (mild salt-cured immature herring) and pickled herring (ättikssill style) are both fermented/salt-cured preparations. High marine omega-3 fat (DHA/EPA) content, moderate IMP. Used as smørrebrød topping, pickled condiment, and festival food.
data_sources: Ackman & Takeuchi (1986) — Fatty acids of Atlantic herring, Lipids 21:117
Nishimura & Kato (1988) — Taste compounds in meat and fish, J Food Sci 53:1217

staple_foods.name: Gouda cheese (aged)

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.5-1.0 g/100g [aged Gouda — residual lactic acid; Kosikowski & Mistry 1997]
organic_acids.propionic_acid: trace [Gouda does not use Propionibacterium unlike Swiss cheese]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.8-1.8 g/100g free glutamate [aged Gouda 18+ month — Weimer et al. 2016 J Dairy Sci; proteolysis-driven]

staple_foods.cultivar: Bos taurus (Holstein, Dutch lowland milk — Gouda PDO)
staple_foods.notes: Aged Gouda from Dutch lowland Holstein milk. Washed-curd semi-hard cheese aged 18+ months (Old Amsterdam style) produces significant free glutamate from proteolysis. The fat fraction reflects the lowland conventional dairy feed — lower CLA than Alpine Gruyère (RSU-36). Provides a direct comparison with RSU-36 alpine Gruyère for the altitude-forage-dairy fat signal.
data_sources: Kosikowski & Mistry (1997) — Cheese and Fermented Milk Foods, vol 1

staple_foods.name: dill (Anethum graveolens)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 600-1200 mg GAE/100g [dill herb — flavonoids and hydroxycinnamic acids; Zheljazkov et al. 2008]

terpenes.limonene: 20-50% of dill EO [Anethum graveolens EO — Singh & Shushni 2011 J Agric Food Chem 59:6301; dominant terpene]

staple_foods.notes: Dill is the defining aromatic herb of Nordic cuisine — the ubiquitous flavoring of pickled fish, potato dishes, and fermented dairy. The limonene-dominant essential oil (20-50% limonene) creates the characteristic fresh, clean, slightly citrus-anise aroma. Used in vast quantities in Danish, Swedish, and Dutch food culture. The high ascorbic acid content (85 mg/100g) reflects the fresh herb's role as a vitamin C source in northern diets.
data_sources: Singh & Shushni (2011) — Chemical composition of dill essential oil, J Agric Food Chem 59:6301

notes: Denmark/Netherlands coastal lowlands (~30m), sedimentary clay-loam soils, temperate maritime climate. The highest agricultural intensification index in this batch — intensive lowland dairy, precision fertilization, year-round silage. Critical function: provides the low-altitude, low-CLA baseline for the butter natural experiment (RSU-43 → RSU-37 → RSU-36 → RSU-42). The contrast between this conventionally-fed lowland dairy fat and the alpine/highland grass-fed systems is the cleanest diet-altitude-terroir experiment in the dataset. Dill adds the most Nordic-specific terpene fingerprint (limonene-dominant EO) in the dataset.
